chunk_summary: Caregiver teach-back ensures safe feeding practices for children with neurodisability, especially during intercurrent illness, emphasizing IDDSI levels, stop criteria, and emergency triggers.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of caregiver teach-back in feeding support for children with neurodisability.
Learn the minimum competencies required for safe feeding, including IDDSI level preparation and stop criteria recognition.
Identify safe feeding modifications during intercurrent illness and triggers for urgent care.
Develop an illness plan tailored to local feasibility, such as India-specific emergency protocols.
key_facts: Teach-back requires caregivers to demonstrate skills and state stop criteria in their own words before discharge or plan changes.
Minimum competencies include correct positioning, IDDSI level preparation (e.g., syringe flow test), and bolus size/pacing rules.
Stop signs include wet voice, persistent cough, distress, drowsiness, and increased work of breathing.
During intercurrent illness, simplify to the safest previously tolerated IDDSI level, reduce bolus volumes, and shift hydration to tube route if coughing with sips begins.
Urgent care triggers include rising respiratory rate, oxygen saturation decline, and inability to maintain hydration.
India-specific illness plans should include feeding route, tube type, and approved temporary hydration shifts.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: "Teach-back: a systematic review of implementation and impacts" highlights the importance of caregivers demonstrating understanding and skills to ensure safe and effective care delivery. (Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7156054/)
document_key_topics: Caregiver teach-back checklist for feeding support
IDDSI level preparation and testing methods
Emergency care triggers and illness plans
